packTitle: Shelter, Fire, and Warmth Import Batch
notes: Normalized from Perplexity category output for Shelter, Fire, and Warmth.
Card-first structure preserved for quick emergency lookup with expandable detail later.
Practical tarp/fire-construction items are included but marked needs_source_review where source strength is more field-practice than agency-standard.
Core indoor warmth, CO safety, heater safety, and shelter-priority guides are marked reviewed and ready for app use.

categories.summary: Normalized shelter, fire, and warmth guides for short-term emergency shelter, cold-weather survival, fire use and safety, and indoor heating/CO risk management.

guides.title: Choosing a Safe Spot for Emergency Shelter
guides.quickAnswer: Pick the safest dry, stable shelter you can reach, away from flood, fire, wind, and falling-debris hazards.
guides.whenToUse: When you must stay put for hours or overnight because of storms, cold, or being stranded.
guides.preferredAction: Choose a solid building first, using a small interior room or protected corner above ground level and away from obvious hazards.
guides.backupAction: If no building is safe, use a vehicle or natural windbreak while avoiding flood channels, unstable slopes, and dead branches.
steps: Scan for the nearest intact shelter or protected spot.
Avoid low areas, riverbanks, rockfall zones, and avalanche or landslide paths.
Choose a small area out of wind and away from broken glass or loose debris.
Get off bare ground with pads, packs, cardboard, or spare clothing.
Keep exits clear and plan how to leave quickly if conditions worsen.
warnings: Damaged buildings after storms or earthquakes may collapse without warning.
whatNotToDo: Do not shelter in basements or low spots if flood risk exists.
Do not shelter under trees in severe wind or lightning.
redFlags: Cracking sounds, sagging roof, or new wall cracks.
Rising water inside the shelter area.
Strong gas smell or smoke.
preparednessTips: Know nearby public shelters or warming centers in advance.
Keep a small grab kit with hat, gloves, light, snacks, and water.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-health guidance on safe sheltering and cold-weather preparation.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical outdoor shelter and exposure-reduction advice.

guides.title: Simple No-Tools Emergency Shelter Inside Buildings
guides.quickAnswer: Close off one small interior room, block drafts, and build a warm “nest” with layers above and below you.
guides.whenToUse: During power outages or heating failure when you can remain inside a building.
guides.preferredAction: Use one small room, seal drafts, insulate the floor, and keep everyone in layered clothing and blankets.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot dedicate a room, create a smaller warm corner with furniture, mattresses, and blankets.
steps: Move everyone into one small room away from exterior doors if possible.
Close doors, blinds, and curtains; block drafts under doors and leaky windows.
Hang blankets, tarps, or plastic sheeting to reduce the space you need to warm.
Put insulation on the floor using mats, cardboard, cushions, or folded clothing.
Wear hats, socks, and layers; share blankets and stay fed and hydrated if safe water is available.
warnings: People can become hypothermic indoors if they are wet, underdressed, or immobile in very cold rooms.
whatNotToDo: Do not use ovens, grills, camp stoves, generators, or unvented fuel devices indoors for heat.
redFlags: Uncontrollable shivering, slurred speech, confusion, or drowsiness.
Indoor temperatures near freezing with no safe heat option.
preparednessTips: Keep extra blankets, foam pads, hats, gloves, and duct tape accessible to quickly warm one room.

sourceReferences.title: Winter at Home – Staying Warm Without Power
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concrete actions for staying warm indoors during outages.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Home preparation and cold-health risk guidance.

guides.title: Basic Outdoor Emergency Shelter Without a Tent
guides.quickAnswer: Get off wet ground, reduce wind exposure, and create simple overhead cover with whatever materials you have.
guides.whenToUse: When stranded outdoors for many hours or overnight without a tent, especially in cold, wet, or windy conditions.
guides.preferredAction: Use a tarp or plastic sheet with cord to make a low, wind-sheltered lean-to or A-frame near a natural windbreak.
guides.backupAction: If you have no sheet or tarp, use branches, leaves, and other natural materials to build a simple debris shelter that blocks wind and insulates you from the ground.
steps: Choose a spot above drainage channels and away from falling branches or rockfall.
Use trees, rocks, or trekking poles as supports for a tarp or sheet; pitch it low and tight with the open side away from wind.
Build a thick insulating layer under you using dry leaves, pine boughs, packs, or spare clothing.
Keep the shelter small so body heat can warm the space more effectively.
Keep your core and head insulated and change out of wet clothes if you have dry layers.
warnings: Improvised shelters offer limited protection; hypothermia is still possible in wet, windy, or very cold conditions.
whatNotToDo: Do not build in dry riverbeds, on ridge tops in storms, or under dead branches (“widow-makers”).
redFlags: Numb hands or feet progressing to clumsiness or confusion.
Inability to stop shivering after shelter and dry layers.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one emergency tarp or space blanket, cord, and a foam pad in outdoor kits.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes shelter, insulation, and wind protection.

sourceReferences.title: Blue FEMA-Spec’d Tarps
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Describes tarps designed for temporary human shelter and emergency roof sheeting.

guides.title: Fast Tarp Lean-To for Wind and Rain
guides.quickAnswer: Pitch a tarp as a low lean-to facing away from the wind, with thick insulation underneath you.
guides.whenToUse: For short-term protection from wind and rain when you need quick setup and access to the outside.
guides.preferredAction: Anchor the top edge between two fixed points and keep the windward side low to block weather.
guides.backupAction: If anchors are poor, use poles, paddles, or sturdy branches and weigh them down with rocks or packs.
steps: Face your back to the wind and find two anchors 2–3 meters apart.
Tie a ridgeline between them at about chest height.
Drape the tarp over the ridgeline so one side reaches the ground on the windward side.
Stake or weigh down the ground edge and angle the open side to shed rain while allowing ventilation.
Build a thick insulating layer underneath where you will sit or lie.
warnings: A lean-to blocks wind on one side only; in very cold or windy conditions a lower A-frame may be warmer.
whatNotToDo: Do not fully seal all edges in cold, damp conditions; trapped moisture can make you colder.
redFlags: Dropping temperature, stronger wind, or soaking wetness that you cannot control with shelter and layers.
preparednessTips: Practice one or two standard tarp pitches in good weather before you need them.

sourceReferences.title: Blue FEMA-Spec’d Tarps
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms tarps’ emergency-shelter role.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Stresses wind, rain, and ground insulation.

guides.title: Low A-Frame Tarp Shelter for Cold Nights
guides.quickAnswer: Pitch the tarp low in an A-frame with both sides close to the ground to conserve heat and block wind.
guides.whenToUse: Overnight in cold, breezy, or light-snow conditions when you need more enclosure than a lean-to.
guides.preferredAction: Use a strong ridgeline and pull both tarp sides down close to the ground, leaving only small gaps for airflow.
guides.backupAction: If stakes are unavailable, use rocks, logs, or filled bags to hold the tarp edges down.
steps: Choose slightly elevated ground with a natural windbreak.
Tie a ridgeline between two anchors at knee-to-thigh height.
Center the tarp over the ridgeline and pull both sides down toward the ground.
Stake or weigh down the edges to create a low triangular tunnel, leaving small openings at each end for condensation control.
Lay thick insulation underneath and sleep away from the open ends.
warnings: Low shelters can collect condensation; wet gear and clothing reduce insulation and increase hypothermia risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not light a fire inside or directly under the tarp; tarps are flammable and smoke will build up quickly.
redFlags: Progressive cold, drowsiness, or confusion despite shelter and dry layers.
preparednessTips: Carry a ground pad and emergency bivy or space blanket to boost warmth inside tarp shelters.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Weather Camping and Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical guidance on keeping shelters dry and insulated.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes insulation and wind-blocking.

guides.title: Using Buildings and Tarps for Temporary Roof and Wall Protection
guides.quickAnswer: Use heavy-duty tarps to cover damaged roofs or broken windows, but stay off unsafe structures.
guides.whenToUse: After storms or earthquakes when a building is mostly sound but has leaks or broken openings.
guides.preferredAction: Cover small to moderate roof or wall damage with a properly anchored tarp to keep the interior dry until repairs are possible.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot safely access the damage, confine living space to dry rooms and use containers to catch leaks.
steps: Inspect roof and walls from the ground first; avoid climbing if anything looks unstable.
For reachable roof leaks, spread a tarp over the damage and extend it well past the leak.
Secure the tarp tightly with ropes, sandbags, or boards so it does not flap loose in the wind.
For broken windows, tape plastic sheeting or a tarp over the opening from the inside and seal the edges.
Keep one clear exit path and do not block vents needed by fuel-burning appliances.
warnings: Working on damaged roofs causes many post-disaster injuries; falls and structural collapse are serious risks.
whatNotToDo: Do not walk on obviously damaged roofs.
Do not cover chimneys or flue outlets that appliances need for safe venting.
redFlags: Sagging roof, large cracks, or leaning walls.
Strong gas smell or visible sparking wires near damage.
preparednessTips: Store at least one heavy-duty tarp, rope, and duct tape if you live in storm-prone areas.
Know whether local “blue roof” or similar programs operate in your area.

sourceReferences.title: Blue FEMA-Spec’d Tarps
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Details tarp design for emergency shelter and roof sheeting.

guides.title: Staying Warm in a Cold Home Without Power
guides.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat
guides.quickAnswer: Gather everyone in one small room, block drafts, insulate floors and windows, and use safe blankets and clothing—not fuel devices—to stay warm.
guides.whenToUse: Any winter power outage or heating-system failure when indoor temperatures are dropping.
guides.preferredAction: Shrink your living space, insulate yourself and the room, and use body heat, layers, and safe sleeping setups.
guides.backupAction: If your home cannot be kept reasonably warm, move to a safer location such as a friend’s house, public shelter, or warming center if travel is safe.
steps: Choose a small room and close off unused areas.
Cover windows, close curtains, and block drafts under doors.
Insulate the floor where you sit and sleep with rugs, mats, cardboard, or cushions.
Wear multiple layers including hat, scarf, gloves, and warm socks; change out of damp clothes.
Eat regular meals and drink fluids; check vulnerable household members often.
warnings: Cold homes can lead to hypothermia, especially in infants, older adults, and people with chronic illness.
whatNotToDo: Do not use ovens, stovetops, camp stoves, charcoal grills, or generators indoors for heat.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, drowsiness, or very cold skin.
Headache, dizziness, or nausea while a fuel-burning device is nearby (possible CO exposure).
preparednessTips: Keep extra blankets, warm clothing, and a battery or crank radio.
Know where local warming centers are announced.

sourceReferences.title: Winter at Home – Staying Warm Without Power
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical actions for staying warm indoors during outages.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Guidance on staying warm and avoiding cold-related illness.

guides.title: Layering Clothing and Bedding for Maximum Warmth
guides.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat
guides.quickAnswer: Use several loose, dry layers and insulate underneath as well as on top to retain heat.
guides.whenToUse: Any cold-weather situation indoors or outdoors, especially overnight.
guides.preferredAction: Use a moisture-wicking inner layer, insulating middle layers, and an outer layer that blocks wind or moisture.
guides.backupAction: If you lack ideal gear, improvise with multiple dry shirts, pants, blankets, and good coverage of head, neck, hands, and feet.
steps: Put on a thin moisture-wicking base layer if available.
Add one or more insulating layers such as fleece or wool.
Use an outer layer that blocks wind and sheds precipitation when outdoors.
For sleeping, put insulation under you and then cover with blankets or sleeping bags on top.
Adjust layers to avoid sweating and change out damp layers as soon as possible.
warnings: Cotton holds moisture and can make you colder when wet in outdoor settings.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore wet socks, gloves, or base layers.
redFlags: Shivering, clumsiness, or confusion that continue after adding layers and shelter.
preparednessTips: Keep at least one non-cotton base layer, warm hat, gloves, and extra socks per person in emergency kits.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear layering and fabric guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Recommends appropriate winter clothing and layering.

guides.title: Keeping Children, Elderly, and Frail Adults Warm Safely
guides.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat
guides.quickAnswer: Keep vulnerable people in the warmest room, use safe layers and blankets, check them often, and avoid unsafe heating methods.
guides.whenToUse: Cold homes, power outages, or outdoor delays involving infants, older adults, or people with illness or disability.
guides.preferredAction: Move vulnerable people to the warmest sealed room, keep them off the floor, and monitor for early signs of hypothermia.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot keep them safely warm, relocate them to a friend’s home, healthcare facility, or official shelter if possible.
steps: Move vulnerable people to the warmest, best-sealed room and keep them off the floor.
Dress them in multiple layers and cover head, hands, and feet.
Offer warm drinks if they are awake and can swallow safely.
Check for unusual fatigue, confusion, slurred speech, or very cold skin.
Keep heaters and open flames away from bedding and clothing.
warnings: Children, older adults, and people with chronic illness can develop hypothermia faster and may not report symptoms clearly.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on hot water bottles, electric blankets, or heaters directly against skin for people who cannot move away or feel burns.
Do not use unsafe fuel-burning heaters indoors.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, extreme sleepiness, or bluish/pale very cold skin.
preparednessTips: Plan in advance where vulnerable household members could stay if your home becomes too cold.
Ensure smoke and CO alarms work and have backup power.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Notes higher-risk groups and protective steps.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety: Get Ready for Cooler Weather
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes safe heating and alarms.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Warns against indoor fuel-burning devices.

guides.title: Keeping Dry in Rain and Snow Outdoors
guides.quickAnswer: Use water-resistant outer layers, protect hands and feet, and minimize time in heavy rain or slush to prevent dangerous chilling.
guides.whenToUse: Outdoor travel or work in wet, cold weather, especially when returning to shelter may be delayed.
guides.preferredAction: Wear waterproof or water-resistant outer layers, keep spare dry socks and gloves, and avoid soaking your clothing when possible.
guides.backupAction: If you lack waterproof gear, improvise with ponchos or plastic over critical areas and reduce exposure time.
steps: Put on or improvise a water-resistant shell over insulating layers.
Protect shoes and socks and change into dry socks when you can.
Keep gloves or mittens as dry as possible and carry a spare pair if available.
Limit time standing in slush, puddles, or wet snow.
Once under shelter, remove wet outer layers and hang them to dry.
warnings: Wet clothing greatly increases heat loss and hypothermia risk in cold or windy weather.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking in wet cotton layers if you can change.
Do not ignore wet feet and hands.
redFlags: Shivering that does not stop after shelter and dry clothes, or progressing clumsiness or confusion.
preparednessTips: Carry simple rain protection and at least one spare pair of socks during cold seasons.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Urges staying dry to prevent heat loss.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes proper clothing and staying dry.

guides.title: Drying Wet Clothing and Bedding Safely Indoors
guides.quickAnswer: Hang wet items spread out in the warmest, best-ventilated space you have, away from heaters or flames that could ignite them.
guides.whenToUse: After getting wet during cold weather once you are in an indoor or sheltered area.
guides.preferredAction: Use clotheslines, hangers, or chairs to air-dry items while keeping them at least three feet from heaters.
guides.backupAction: If heat is limited, prioritize drying socks, gloves, and base layers; wring items thoroughly and rotate them closer to safe heat sources while supervising constantly.
steps: Wring out wet clothes and bedding away from sleeping areas.
Hang items so air can circulate; avoid piling wet items in heaps.
Keep all fabrics at least three feet away from heaters, stoves, or open flames.
Ventilate briefly if possible to reduce dampness and mold risk.
Put on dry layers while items dry rather than waiting in wet clothing.
warnings: Clothing or bedding too close to heaters is a common cause of home fires.
whatNotToDo: Do not drape clothes directly on heaters or over open-flame devices.
Do not leave heaters unattended while drying items.
redFlags: Smoke, scorch marks, burning smell, or any small fire that is not immediately controllable.
preparednessTips: Keep clips or rope for indoor drying lines and maintain working smoke alarms near heating areas.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Stresses keeping combustibles three feet from heat sources.

sourceReferences.title: Safety Tips: Portable Space Heaters
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical heater safety distances and guidance.

guides.title: Managing Moisture Inside Tents and Improvised Shelters
guides.quickAnswer: Ventilate enough to reduce condensation while keeping wind and rain out, and keep sleeping areas off wet ground.
guides.whenToUse: When sleeping in tents or tarp shelters in damp, cold conditions.
guides.preferredAction: Use ground insulation, open small vents or gaps, and keep wet gear separate from sleeping areas.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot ventilate well, wipe condensation periodically and rotate damp items out of sleeping areas.
steps: Pitch on slightly higher ground to avoid pooling water.
Use a groundsheet or tarp under the sleeping area, slightly smaller than the tent floor.
Open small vents or gaps at the top or downwind side to let moisture escape.
Keep wet clothing and boots away from sleeping insulation.
Wipe interior condensation with a cloth if it builds up.
warnings: Humidity and condensation can chill you overnight and dampen insulation, increasing hypothermia risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not fully seal all vents while cooking or using fuel appliances in or near the shelter.
redFlags: Drowsiness, headaches, or nausea while any fuel-burning device is in use nearby.
preparednessTips: Carry a small groundsheet and cloth for wiping condensation; learn your tent vents before emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Weather Camping and Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Discusses keeping tents dry and managing moisture.

guides.title: Recognizing and Responding to Hypothermia
guides.quickAnswer: Watch for shivering, confusion, slurred speech, and clumsiness; get the person dry, insulated, sheltered, and seek medical help for moderate or worse symptoms.
guides.whenToUse: Any cold exposure, especially when people are wet, tired, or underdressed.
guides.preferredAction: Stop further cooling, gently rewarm with dry clothing and insulation, and seek care early.
guides.backupAction: If medical help is delayed, keep the person horizontal, insulated above and below, and warm them gradually, especially the core.
steps: Check for early signs such as intense shivering, cold pale skin, numb hands or feet, and mild confusion.
Move the person to shelter away from wind and wet conditions.
Remove wet clothing and replace with dry layers; wrap in blankets or a sleeping bag including head and neck.
Offer warm, sweet drinks if they are awake and can swallow.
Seek emergency care for worsening confusion, stiff muscles, severe drowsiness, or slowed breathing.
warnings: Moderate to severe hypothermia is a medical emergency; rough handling can trigger dangerous heart problems.
whatNotToDo: Do not give alcohol.
Do not put the person in a hot bath or directly against a heater.
Do not massage cold limbs vigorously.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, inability to walk normally, slowed pulse or breathing, or unconsciousness.
preparednessTips: Learn hypothermia signs before trips and carry extra clothing, emergency blankets, and warm drink supplies.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Hypothermia signs and response.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed prevention and field treatment advice.

guides.title: Emergency Overnight Setup When You Are Already Wet
guides.quickAnswer: Get under cover, remove wet layers if possible, insulate yourself from the ground, and warm your core with dry clothes or shared insulation.
guides.whenToUse: Unexpected overnight in cold, wet conditions where you or others are already soaked.
guides.preferredAction: Use any available shelter, change into dry layers, and use dry blankets or sleeping bags to protect the core first.
guides.backupAction: If no dry clothes exist, wring out wet layers, put them back on, and maximize insulation with natural materials and shared coverings.
steps: Move immediately to the best available shelter.
Remove wet upper-body clothing and replace it with anything dry; protect the head and torso well.
Lay down thick insulation under you using packs, branches, leaves, cardboard, or extra clothing.
Wrap in all available blankets, sleeping bags, or dry fabrics.
Eat and drink if safe to fuel heat production and avoid more exposure outside the shelter.
warnings: Remaining in wet clothing in cold conditions can lead to hypothermia even above freezing temperatures.
whatNotToDo: Do not sit or sleep directly on bare ground or metal.
Do not rely only on a fire if building one would be slow, unsafe, or impossible.
redFlags: Any signs of moderate hypothermia such as confusion, slurred speech, or stumbling despite warming efforts.
preparednessTips: Pack at least one fully dry emergency set of base layers and socks in a waterproof bag for cold/wet trips.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Advises staying dry and warm.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Highlights changing out of wet clothing and insulation.

guides.title: Overnight Survival in a Vehicle in Cold Weather
guides.quickAnswer: Stay in the vehicle, insulate yourself, run the engine only periodically with a clear exhaust pipe, and avoid sleeping with continuous engine or heater use.
guides.whenToUse: When stranded in a vehicle during winter storms or extreme cold.
guides.preferredAction: Remain in the vehicle, keep visible to rescuers, and manage warmth with clothing, blankets, and carefully limited engine use.
guides.backupAction: If staying is unsafe, such as due to buried exhaust, fuel leak, or another immediate hazard, move to a safer nearby shelter only if you can do so without getting lost or badly exposed.
steps: Stay with your vehicle; it is easier to find than a person on foot.
Check that the exhaust pipe is not blocked by snow or debris before running the engine.
Run the engine and heater only long enough to warm the interior, then shut it off to conserve fuel and reduce CO risk.
Open a window slightly on the side away from the wind for ventilation.
Wear all available layers and use blankets, sleeping bags, or extra clothing for insulation.
warnings: Blocked exhaust pipes or running engines in enclosed spaces can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning.
whatNotToDo: Do not sleep with the engine running continuously.
Do not use charcoal grills or camp stoves inside the vehicle.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion while the engine is running.
Snow building up around the vehicle.
preparednessTips: Keep a winter car kit with blankets, hats, gloves, shovel, traction aid, food, water, and flashlight.
Maintain at least a half tank of fuel in winter.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Specific advice on staying in vehicles and checking exhaust pipes.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Explains CO dangers from engines and fuel devices.

guides.title: Basic Outdoor Fire Starting with Matches or Lighters
guides.quickAnswer: Prepare a safe fire site, build a small structure of tinder, kindling, and fuel, then light the tinder and add wood gradually.
guides.whenToUse: Outdoor situations where a fire is allowed and safe, for warmth, signaling, or boiling water.
guides.preferredAction: Use dry fine tinder, small kindling, and then larger fuel, protecting the flame from wind and keeping the fire small.
guides.backupAction: If matches or a lighter are weak, use multiple small tinder bundles and shield them carefully while lighting.
steps: Confirm local rules and safety before building a fire.
Clear the fire area down to mineral soil and away from overhanging branches, tents, and flammables.
Gather three fuel sizes: tinder, kindling, and larger fuel wood.
Arrange tinder in a small bundle and build kindling around it with space for air.
Light the tinder from the windward side and add more kindling, then larger wood, as the fire strengthens.
warnings: Even small fires can spread quickly in dry or windy conditions; embers can ignite nearby vegetation.
whatNotToDo: Do not use gasoline or unknown flammable liquids.
Do not leave a fire unattended or build it against roots, logs, or peat soils.
redFlags: Flames spreading beyond your cleared area or wind pushing sparks toward structures or vegetation.
preparednessTips: Carry waterproof matches or a lighter and reliable tinder in a waterproof container when traveling outdoors.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor fire-building guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common safety-focused outdoor fire-starting practice, adapted to local rules.

guides.title: Safe Fire Site Selection and Wind Management
guides.quickAnswer: Choose an open, clear area away from vegetation, roots, and overhead branches, and account for wind so sparks and smoke blow away from people and shelter.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you plan to make an outdoor fire.
guides.preferredAction: Use existing fire rings or designated sites whenever possible; otherwise clear down to mineral soil and keep a bare radius around the fire.
guides.backupAction: If the ground cannot be cleared safely, skip the fire and rely on shelter, clothing, and hot drinks instead.
steps: Look up and around; avoid branches, tents, tarps, and dry grass.
Select bare soil, sand, or gravel if possible and clear a 1–2 meter radius of flammable material.
Note wind direction and keep sparks blowing into nonflammable areas.
Keep water, dirt, or sand nearby to extinguish the fire quickly.
Keep the fire small and manageable.
warnings: Wildfire risk can be extreme in dry or windy conditions; authorities may restrict all open flame.
whatNotToDo: Do not build fires in high winds, during fire bans, inside or under shelters, or directly on peat or organic soils.
redFlags: Rapidly moving grass fire, uncontrolled flames, or smoke threatening people or structures.
preparednessTips: Check local fire danger ratings and learn at least one warming method that does not rely on open flame.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor fire-safety principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Standard fire-agency messaging on site selection, spark control, and fire bans.

guides.title: Choosing and Preparing Dry Fuel
guides.quickAnswer: Collect small, dead, and dry wood from off the ground and sheltered areas, and split larger pieces to expose drier inner wood if you have the skill and tools.
guides.whenToUse: Any outdoor fire building, especially after rain or snow.
guides.preferredAction: Gather a generous supply of small, dry fuel before lighting; prioritize dead branches from standing trees and sheltered spots.
guides.backupAction: If most wood is damp, split thicker pieces to access drier cores and use extra tinder and kindling.
steps: Look for dead branches still on trees or under logs and rocks where they stayed drier.
Snap small twigs; if they break crisply, they are more likely to be dry enough.
Collect plenty of kindling and medium sticks before lighting.
If experienced and properly equipped, split thicker pieces to expose dry inner surfaces.
Keep your driest fuel under cover once collected.
warnings: Handling cutting tools when cold and tired increases injury risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not cut live trees or branches in protected areas.
Do not rely on a tiny fuel pile and assume you can gather more after dark.
redFlags: You cannot find or prepare enough dry fuel to stay warm and are already cold and wet.
preparednessTips: Carry a small amount of known-dry fuel or prepared tinder where wood may be scarce or wet.

sourceReferences.title: Fuel-selection strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely taught fuel-selection and preparation techniques for outdoor fires.

guides.title: Finding and Using Dry Tinder in Wet Weather
guides.quickAnswer: Look in sheltered places for the driest fine material, and use more tinder than usual to overcome dampness.
guides.whenToUse: Rain, snow, or soaked ground make normal fire starting difficult, but a fire is still safe and allowed.
guides.preferredAction: Use carried tinder plus the driest inner wood or bark you can process into fine shavings.
guides.backupAction: If you have no carried tinder, collect dry inner wood, dead standing twigs, and fibrous bark and process them into thin curls or fibers.
steps: Check your kit first for dry tinder stored in waterproof packaging.
Look under logs, under overhangs, or inside dead standing wood for dry material.
Shave or scrape dry inner wood or bark into thin curls or fibers.
Build a large tinder bundle, then surround it with very fine kindling.
Shield your tinder from rain with your body, pack, or tarp while lighting.
warnings: Fire starting in wet conditions is skill-intensive and may fail; do not rely on it as your only survival plan.
whatNotToDo: Do not strip bark from live trees in protected areas.
Do not waste your only ignition source on poorly prepared tinder.
redFlags: Repeated failed attempts while you are becoming colder and wetter.
preparednessTips: Always carry some dry tinder and a waterproof ignition source; do not assume you can improvise in all conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Field-oriented hypothermia prevention guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports prioritizing dryness and shelter and acknowledges the difficulty of wet-weather fire starting.

guides.title: Building a Fire Lay That Survives Rain and Snow
guides.quickAnswer: Raise the fire off soaked ground, protect it from above, and stack fuel so it can dry and catch as the fire grows.
guides.whenToUse: When the ground is wet or snow-covered but you still need and are allowed to build a fire.
guides.preferredAction: Create a small platform of sticks or rocks, build your tinder and kindling on top, and use overhead cover only if it can be kept well away from flames.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot provide overhead cover, build a compact fire with a windward log wall and keep it small and controlled.
steps: Lay a base of small sticks or rocks to raise the fire off wet ground.
Place tinder on the platform and arrange kindling in a teepee or log-cabin style.
Stack larger damp fuel nearby so it can pre-dry from the heat once the fire is established.
If using a tarp or poncho for rain protection, keep it high and far enough from heat to avoid melting or ignition.
Keep the fire small and compact for better heat and fuel efficiency.
warnings: Using overhead plastic or fabric covers near open flame is risky and must be constantly supervised.
whatNotToDo: Do not build large fires in wet or windy conditions just because they are harder to start.
redFlags: Spreading flames into nearby vegetation or inability to control the fire due to wind or fuel conditions.
preparednessTips: Practice building small, efficient fires in safe settings before relying on them in emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: Practical field fire-lay techniques
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common wet-ground and wet-weather fire-lay techniques taught in field courses.

guides.title: When Not to Attempt a Fire in Wet Conditions
guides.quickAnswer: Skip building a fire if it is unsafe, banned, or would distract from higher-priority tasks like shelter and getting dry.
guides.whenToUse: Any wet, cold situation where building a fire is difficult or risky.
guides.preferredAction: Focus first on shelter, dry clothing, insulation, and food; treat fire as a backup tool only when conditions and safety allow.
guides.backupAction: Use chemical heat packs, movement if safe, or shared insulation if available.
steps: Check local fire restrictions and evaluate wildfire risk.
Assess your skills, tools, and available dry tinder and fuel honestly.
If risk or difficulty is high, spend your time improving shelter, clothing, and bedding instead.
Maintain morale and body temperature with movement, warm drinks if possible, and social support.
warnings: Spending hours trying to start a fire in harsh conditions can leave you colder, wetter, and more exhausted.
whatNotToDo: Do not try risky improvisations with accelerants or unknown fuels out of frustration.
redFlags: You are wet, cold, unable to stop shivering, and lack safe fire or better shelter options.
preparednessTips: Plan so you rarely depend on fire alone: carry proper clothing, shelter, and emergency warming supplies.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia prevention principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports prioritizing shelter, dryness, and insulation over marginal fire attempts.

guides.title: Fully Extinguishing Outdoor Fires
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guides.quickAnswer: Drown the fire with water, stir the ashes, and repeat until everything is cold to the touch; never leave warm or smoking material behind.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you are done with an outdoor fire or must leave it unattended.
guides.preferredAction: Use plenty of water and stirring to cool all coals, not just visible flames.
guides.backupAction: If water is scarce, use dirt or sand while mixing thoroughly, then check for remaining heat; avoid relying on soil alone in peat or root systems.
steps: Let the fire burn down to ash and small coals if time allows.
Pour water over the entire fire area, including logs and surrounding ground.
Stir with a stick or tool, exposing hidden coals; add more water until hissing stops and all material is cool.
If using dirt or sand, mix thoroughly and check again for heat.
Feel carefully near the surface; if it is warm, repeat the cooling steps.
warnings: Poorly extinguished fires can smolder and later ignite wildfires, especially in dry or windy conditions.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave coals or large unburned logs.
Do not bury a hot fire under thick soil without confirming it is cold.
redFlags: Smoke or flames reappearing after you thought the fire was out and beginning to spread beyond your control.
preparednessTips: Carry enough water for extinguishing or avoid fires altogether when water is limited and fire risk is high.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire-prevention fire-out guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative extinguishing practices consistent with park and wildfire-prevention messaging.

guides.title: Safe Use of Portable Heaters Indoors
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guides.quickAnswer: Keep space heaters three feet from anything that can burn, plug them directly into wall outlets, and turn them off when you sleep or leave the room.
guides.whenToUse: Using electric or fuel-fired portable heaters during cold weather or power shortages.
guides.preferredAction: Use modern, certified heaters with tip-over and overheat protection exactly as directed.
guides.backupAction: If you lack safe heaters, rely on clothing, blankets, and room-sealing techniques rather than unsafe heat sources.
steps: Place the heater on a flat, stable, nonflammable surface.
Keep a three-foot kid- and pet-free zone around it.
Plug electric heaters directly into a wall outlet, never a power strip or extension cord.
Turn heaters off and unplug them when leaving the room or going to sleep.
Install and test smoke alarms and carbon monoxide detectors where appropriate.
warnings: Portable heaters are a leading cause of home heating fires and related deaths.
whatNotToDo: Do not use damaged heaters, run cords under rugs, use heaters near water, or use outdoor fuel heaters indoors.
redFlags: Burning smells, sparks, discolored outlets, or any small fire in the heater or nearby materials.
preparednessTips: Inspect heaters before cold seasons and keep alarms working with backup power.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Key portable-heater safety rules.

sourceReferences.title: Safety Tips: Portable Space Heaters
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical heater safety advice.

guides.title: Fuel Storage and Handling During Emergencies
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guides.quickAnswer: Store fuels outside living areas in approved containers, away from heat sources, and never use them indoors for open burning.
guides.whenToUse: Keeping gasoline, propane, or similar fuels on hand for generators, stoves, or vehicles during emergencies.
guides.preferredAction: Follow local rules for approved containers and store fuel outside the home, upright, sealed, and away from ignition sources.
guides.backupAction: If you must temporarily keep small quantities closer, ensure containers are closed, upright, and away from heat and sparks.
steps: Use only approved, labeled fuel containers.
Store fuels outside living spaces in a shed or detached garage if possible.
Keep containers upright, sealed, and out of direct sunlight or high heat.
When refueling generators or stoves, turn them off and let them cool first.
Clean spills immediately and ventilate the area.
warnings: Improper storage raises risks of fire, explosion, and toxic fumes including carbon monoxide when fuels are burned.
whatNotToDo: Do not store fuel next to heaters, fireplaces, or cooking appliances.
Do not use gasoline or similar fuels to start indoor fires or stoves.
redFlags: Strong fuel odors inside the home, visible leaks, or any ignition of spilled fuel.
preparednessTips: Plan fuel needs realistically, avoid hoarding, and learn local storage and transport rules.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Notes CO risks from fuel-burning devices.

guides.title: Safe Options for Heating Indoors During Power Outages
guides.quickAnswer: Use built-in heating systems, properly vented fireplaces or wood stoves, and listed portable electric heaters; avoid unvented fuel devices.
guides.whenToUse: Cold-weather power outages or heating failures when you must stay in place.
guides.preferredAction: Rely on safe, code-compliant heating systems plus room sealing and clothing layers.
guides.backupAction: Use portable electric heaters on generator power only if the generator is outside and all heater safety steps are followed.
steps: Use central heating if it still works safely; otherwise move to a small room and focus on insulation and clothing.
If using a fireplace or wood stove, ensure the chimney or flue is clear and keep combustibles three feet away.
Use only modern listed electric space heaters indoors and follow heater safety rules.
If powering heaters by generator, keep the generator outdoors away from windows and vents and use proper cords.
Monitor carbon monoxide and smoke alarms and never block vents or flues.
warnings: Improper heating is a major cause of home fires and carbon monoxide poisoning during outages and cold spells.
whatNotToDo: Do not use charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens for space heating.
Do not bring generators, patio heaters, or gas heaters indoors.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion during heater use.
Any signs of fire or smoke from heating equipment.
preparednessTips: Have heating systems and chimneys inspected before winter and maintain working smoke and CO detectors.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Indoor heating and CO safety tips.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Safe heater and fireplace use.

guides.title: Unsafe Indoor Heating Methods to Avoid
guides.quickAnswer: Never use generators, charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens to heat your home; they can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning and fires.
guides.whenToUse: Any cold-weather emergency where people might feel tempted to improvise heat indoors.
guides.preferredAction: Reject unsafe methods and instead rely on clothing, bedding, room sealing, and safe heaters or relocation to a warming center.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot stay warm without unsafe methods, move to a safer location if possible.
steps: Identify clearly unsafe indoor heat sources such as generators, camping stoves, charcoal or gas grills, and gas ovens left open.
Commit to not using them inside homes, garages, or near windows and vents.
Use staying-warm-without-power methods instead.
If others suggest unsafe heating, show them clear CO and fire warnings.
warnings: CO is invisible and odorless; many people have died using generators and fuel-burning devices indoors for heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not bring generators inside or run them near windows.
Do not use stoves or ovens for house heating.
Do not sleep in rooms with burning charcoal or unvented fuel devices.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, or confusion when a fuel device is in use nearby.
preparednessTips: Install CO detectors with battery backup and learn which devices are safe only outdoors.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Warns against using generators and grills indoors.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: CO dangers from fuel-burning devices.

guides.title: Recognizing and Responding to Carbon Monoxide Exposure
guides.quickAnswer: If people around a fuel-burning device develop headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion, get everyone into fresh air immediately and call emergency services.
guides.whenToUse: Any time generators, heaters, stoves, or other fuel-burning devices are used, especially during outages.
guides.preferredAction: Prevent CO buildup by keeping fuel devices outside or properly vented and relying on CO alarms as early warning.
guides.backupAction: If no CO alarm is present, watch closely for symptoms appearing in multiple people at once and act quickly if they appear.
steps: Install CO detectors with battery backup on each floor and near sleeping areas; test them regularly.
Never use fuel-burning devices indoors or in attached garages; keep them well away from windows and vents.
If the CO alarm sounds or people feel unwell, turn off possible sources only if you can do so quickly and safely.
Evacuate everyone to fresh air outside, then call emergency services.
Do not re-enter until the building has been checked and declared safe.
warnings: CO causes many emergency visits and deaths each year; symptoms can look like flu or fatigue.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore or silence CO alarms without finding the cause.
Do not sleep near running generators or fuel heaters outside windows.
redFlags: Multiple people with headache, dizziness, nausea, shortness of breath, or confusion in the same area.
Unconsciousness or seizures in anyone.
preparednessTips: Ensure CO alarms are installed and working before winter; have heating systems and chimneys inspected annually.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Symptoms, risks, and prevention advice.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety: Get Ready for Cooler Weather
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Recommends CO detectors and system checks.

guides.title: Deciding When Shelter Matters More Than Fire
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guides.quickAnswer: In most cold emergencies, getting under shelter and into dry, insulated clothing is more reliable and safer than trying to build a fire.
guides.whenToUse: Whenever you are exposed to cold and considering spending time and energy making a fire.
guides.preferredAction: Prioritize wind and rain protection, dry clothing, and insulation from the ground first; treat fire as a supplement.
guides.backupAction: If no shelter or adequate clothing is possible, then consider a carefully chosen, safe fire as an additional heat source where allowed.
steps: Assess wind, wetness, and available shelter options.
Secure shelter and dry layers first, even if simple.
Once reasonably protected, decide whether a fire adds enough benefit to justify the risk and effort.
If you choose to build a fire, follow fire-safety guidance closely.
warnings: Overemphasizing fire starting can waste critical time and energy while you continue losing heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not delay getting under shelter or changing out of wet clothes because you are focused on getting a fire going.
redFlags: Ongoing shivering, confusion, or clumsiness despite shelter and clothing improvements.
preparednessTips: Practice quick shelter setups and carry proper clothing so you do not depend on fire as a primary survival tool.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes staying dry and sheltered.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Highlights clothing, shelter, and moisture management over fire.

guides.title: Prioritizing Tasks in the First 30 Minutes of Exposure
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guides.quickAnswer: Use your first 30 minutes to get out of wind and rain, adjust clothing, protect hands and feet, and secure shelter; leave advanced fire tasks for later.
guides.whenToUse: Onset of any cold exposure emergency such as getting lost, being stranded, or the start of a power outage.
guides.preferredAction: Follow a simple order: location safety, shelter, clothing/layers, insulation, then warmth/food and communication.
guides.backupAction: If you must move because of immediate danger, prioritize reaching safer terrain and minimum shelter before anything else.
steps: Move away from immediate hazards such as rising water, unstable structures, or avalanche paths.
Get under any available shelter.
Adjust or change clothing to a drier layered setup; protect head, hands, and feet.
Insulate from the ground using whatever you have.
Only then think about fire, additional insulation, or signaling for help.
warnings: Poor early decisions such as staying in hazard areas or ignoring wet clothing quickly make later survival tasks harder.
whatNotToDo: Do not wander aimlessly in storms.
Do not spend your first half hour on low-priority tasks while still exposed.
redFlags: Immediate life threats such as rising water, structural collapse, or serious injury take precedence over warmth decisions.
preparednessTips: Mentally rehearse a simple first-30-minutes checklist for your climate and activities and share it with family or group members.

sourceReferences.title: Cold-weather and severe-weather safety principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: App-friendly checklist synthesized from multiple cold-exposure and severe-weather guidance sources.

guides.title: When to Seek Public Shelters or Warming Centers
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guides.quickAnswer: If you cannot keep your home above a safe temperature or must consider unsafe heating methods, go to a public shelter or warming center if travel is safe.
guides.whenToUse: Extended power outages, fuel shortages, or severe cold that overwhelm safe heating options at home.
guides.preferredAction: Monitor local alerts for shelter or warming center locations and go early before conditions worsen.
guides.backupAction: If travel is unsafe, use cold-home strategies while staying in contact with neighbors or authorities.
steps: Follow local emergency alerts for shelter or warming center information.
Decide early whether your home can remain safely habitable.
Gather essential supplies such as medications, IDs, and warm clothing and move to the shelter if travel is safe.
Check on neighbors and vulnerable people who may also need help.
At the shelter, follow staff instructions and keep your personal emergency kit with you.
warnings: Waiting too long can make travel more dangerous and reduce space at shelters.
whatNotToDo: Do not stay in an unheated home and resort to unsafe devices instead of going to a shelter.
Do not ignore official evacuation or shelter guidance.
redFlags: Household members showing hypothermia signs or indoor temperatures approaching freezing with no safe heat source.
preparednessTips: Know in advance how local authorities announce shelters and where common locations are.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Encourages planning for shelters and checking on others.

sourceReferences.title: How to Heat Your Home Safely as Cold Weather Sets In
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Stresses safer alternatives and community support.
